formulas.name: Content
formulas.formula:
Since I couldn't find information on the specific formulas used in CBSE Class 11 Computer Science - Emerging Trends, I'll provide a generic formula sheet that might be useful for the topic. Please note that this is not a comprehensive list and might not cover all the required formulas.

```json
[
  {
    "name": "Algorithm Efficiency",
    "formula": "Big O notation: O(n), O(n log n), O(1), O(log n), O(n^2)",
    "variables": "n - size of input, log n - logarithm of n, 1 - constant factor",
    "units": "",
    "notes": "Used to measure the time and space complexity of algorithms."
  },
  {
    "name": "Time Complexity",
    "formula": "T(n) = a*n^b + c (for n >= n0)",
    "variables": "T(n) - time complexity, a - leading coefficient, n - size of input, b - exponent, c - constant term, n0 - lower bound",
    "units": "seconds, milliseconds, etc.",
    "notes": "Used to analyze the running time of algorithms."
  },
  {
    "name": "Space Complexity",
    "formula": "S(n) = a*n^b + c (for n >= n0)",
    "variables": "S(n) - space complexity, a - leading coefficient, n - size of input, b - exponent, c - constant term, n0 - lower bound",
    "units": "bytes, kilobytes, etc.",
    "notes": "Used to analyze the memory usage of algorithms."
  },
  {
    "name": "Average Time Complexity",
    "formula": "T(n) = (a*n^b + c) / n",
    "variables": "T(n) - average time complexity, a - leading coefficient, n - size of input, b - exponent, c - constant term",
    "units": "seconds, milliseconds, etc.",
    "notes": "Used to analyze the average running time of algorithms."
  },
  {
    "name": "Big Omega Notation",
    "formula": "Ω(n)",
    "variables": "n - size of input",
    "units": "",
    "notes": "Used to represent the lower bound of time or space complexity."
  },
  {
    "name": "Big Theta Notation",
    "formula": "Θ(n)",
    "variables": "n - size of input",
    "units": "",
    "notes": "Used to represent the tight bound of time or space complexity."
  },
  {
    "name": "Master Theorem",
    "formula": "T(n) = a*T(n/b) + f(n)",
    "variables": "T(n) - time complexity, a - leading coefficient, n - size of input, b - exponent, f(n) - auxiliary function",
    "units": "seconds, milliseconds, etc.",
    "notes": "Used to solve recurrence relations."
  },
  {
    "name": "Recurrence Relations",
    "formula": "T(n) = a*T(n/b) + f(n)",
    "variables": "T(n) - time complexity, a - leading coefficient, n - size of input, b - exponent, f(n) - auxiliary function",
    "units": "seconds, milliseconds, etc.",
    "notes": "Used to analyze the running time of recursive algorithms."
  },
  {
    "name": "Sorting Algorithms",
    "formula": "Time complexity: O(n log n), O(n^2), etc.",
    "variables": "n - size of input, log n - logarithm of n",
    "units": "seconds, milliseconds, etc.",
    "notes": "Used to compare the efficiency of different sorting algorithms."
  },
  {
    "name": "Searching Algorithms",
    "formula": "Time complexity: O(n), O(log n), etc.",
    "variables": "n - size of input, log n - logarithm of n",
    "units": "seconds, milliseconds, etc.",
    "notes": "Used to compare the efficiency of different searching algorithms."
  }
]
```

Please note that this is not an exhaustive list and might not cover all the required formulas. You should consult your textbook or instructor for a comprehensive list of formulas and their explanations.
